topic: Cursor Worktrees Guide 2026: Parallel Agents Without File Conflicts

competitors.title: Running Parallel Agents in Cursor with Git Worktree
key_points: Git worktrees allow multiple branches from same repository simultaneously, each in own directory
Cursor's chat uses git worktrees to run multiple agents at same time, each completely isolated
Agent Mode + Plan Mode + worktrees pattern: Plan first, run in isolation, review before landing
Parallel example: authentication agent and notifications agent running simultaneously on separate features
Key benefit: multiple agents don't touch each other's files, no conflicts, review each independently

competitors.title: Worktrees | Cursor Docs
key_points: /worktree for one isolated run: keeps experimental edits away, runs installs/builds/tests without disturbing main branch
/best-of-n to compare multiple models: sends same task to different models in isolated worktrees, pick strongest result
/apply-worktree brings changes back to main checkout, /delete-worktree removes when done
Cursor CLI supports same isolation with global --worktree flag using ~/.cursor/worktrees storage
Each worktree has own files and uncommitted changes, main checkout stays untouched until applying result

competitors.title: Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex are merging into one AI coding stack nobody planned
key_points: Cursor 3 (Glass) launched April 2, 2026 with Agents Window for managing multiple AI agents simultaneously
Developers can run parallel agents across local machines, worktrees, and cloud sandboxes from single sidebar
/best-of-n command sends same prompt to multiple models in isolated worktrees for comparison
Cursor moving away from VS Code towards orchestration layer with editor becoming secondary
Composable AI coding stack: Cursor for orchestration, Claude Code/Codex for execution, cross-provider review

stats.fact: Cursor 3 launched April 2, 2026 with Agents Window dedicated to managing multiple AI agents simultaneously
Survey of 906 software engineers (February 2026): Claude Code most-used AI coding tool with 46% 'most loved' rating
stats.source: Pragmatic Engineer survey via The New Stack

stats.fact: SemiAnalysis estimates Claude Code accounts for roughly 4% of all public GitHub commits (March 2026), projected 20% by year-end
Claude Code annualized revenue estimated over $2.5 billion by March 2026 (analyst estimates)
stats.source: Analyst estimates via The New Stack

stats.fact: Codex recently surpassed 3 million weekly active users, up from 2 million just a month earlier
Worktrees use isolated Git checkouts beside main checkout, allowing multiple agents to work simultaneously without file conflicts
/best-of-n command compares same task across different models in isolated worktrees for optimal approach selection
Combination of Plan Mode + Agent Mode + worktrees allows parallel sessions with confidence: some need planning, others simple fixes

angles: Productivity multiplier: How worktrees enable true parallel development with multiple AI agents simultaneously
Risk management: Using worktrees for experimental edits and risky refactors with simple cleanup path
Model comparison: Leveraging /best-of-n to compare different AI models on same task before committing
Team workflows: How development teams can adopt parallel agent patterns for faster feature delivery
Integration strategy: Where parallel agents fit in existing CI/CD pipelines and code review processes
Error prevention: How isolated worktrees prevent file conflicts and merge headaches in collaborative environments
Future scalability: Anticipating multi-agent workflows as AI coding tools evolve beyond single-agent patterns
outline: Introduction: The power of parallel AI agents in modern development workflows
Understanding Git worktrees: Simultaneous branch checkouts without cloning
The Agents Window: Cursor 3's dedicated interface for managing multiple agents
Practical workflow: Authentication vs Notification agents running in parallel
Plan Mode + Agent Mode + worktrees pattern for confident parallel sessions
Model comparison strategies: Using /best-of-n to pick optimal approach
Risk management: Experimental edits and refactors in isolated environments
Team adoption patterns: Scaling parallel agents across development teams
Integration with existing workflows: CI/CD, code review, and deployment pipelines
Common pitfalls: File conflicts, merge strategies, and cleanup best practices
Performance considerations: Resource usage and efficiency gains
Future trends: Multi-agent ecosystems and AI orchestration platforms
Comparison: Cursor worktrees vs Claude Code multi-agent approaches
Tool selection: When to use Cursor vs other AI coding assistants for parallel work
Conclusion: The emerging pattern of parallel AI agent development
